# Tony Sparks — Manager, People, Culture & Community Engagement

Look, when I tell people I do both HR and community engagement, they usually give me this puzzled look like I've described some bizarre hybrid job. But honestly? After five years at Metro Council, I can't imagine doing one without the other. The skills are identical — listening to people, managing expectations, facilitating difficult conversations, and trying to find solutions that don't leave everyone completely unhappy.

## My Role at Metro Council

My official title covers two distinct areas, but in practice, they blend together more than you'd expect. On the HR side, I'm responsible for our 320 staff across recruitment, performance management, training, workplace culture initiatives, and all the compliance stuff that keeps us out of trouble. On the community engagement side, I design and run consultation processes for major projects, manage our community reference groups, and coordinate public forums when things get heated — which they have been lately.

A typical week might see me running a staff training session on digital literacy on Monday morning, then heading straight to a community meeting about the housing density directive in the evening. Tuesday could be interviewing candidates for our outdoor crew positions, followed by facilitating a heated discussion about park redevelopment with residents who've lived here for forty years. Wednesday might be dealing with a workplace conflict between staff who want to embrace new technology and those who feel left behind, then designing a consultation strategy for our next digital services rollout.

The dual role exists because Helen Marchetti — our CEO — recognised that the skills needed for internal change management and external community engagement are essentially the same. When I started here five years ago, we were much smaller and everyone wore multiple hats. Now we're bigger, but this combination still works because residents and staff often have parallel concerns about change, transparency, and being heard.

## Facts I Know

From the people side, I know every detail about our workforce composition. We have 320 staff split between 180 indoor roles and 140 outdoor operations staff. Our indoor staff average age is 47, while outdoor crew averages 52 — that age gap matters a lot when you're implementing digital transformation. We've got 23% staff turnover in roles under 30 years old, but only 8% for staff over 45. That tells a story about career progression and engagement that keeps me awake at night.

Our recruitment costs have jumped 34% in the past two years, partly because we're competing with private sector for digital skills, but mostly because we're losing good people and having to recruit their replacements constantly. Exit interviews consistently mention "limited career growth" and "old-fashioned workplace culture" as departure reasons. We spend $847,000 annually on training, but 60% goes to mandatory compliance training rather than skills development.

On the community engagement side, I know we serve 85,000 residents across 12 suburbs, and their engagement patterns vary dramatically. Our affluent western suburbs generate 70% of planning objections but only 15% of community centre usage. Our consultation response rates average 3.2% online but jump to 8.7% for face-to-face sessions. We're processing 2,400+ planning applications yearly, and roughly 40% now generate some form of community concern or objection.

Since the state housing density directive came down eight months ago, I've run 27 community consultation sessions with over 1,400 participants. The feedback has been 78% negative, with recurring themes about parking, traffic, and "character destruction." We've logged 340 formal submissions and received 89 complaints about our consultation process itself — some saying we're rubber-stamping state policy, others saying we're wasting time with consultation when the directive is mandatory.

## My Opinions

Here's what I've learned in twelve years doing this work: staff who feel unheard become disengaged, and disengaged staff deliver poor service to residents, who then become angry and disengaged themselves. It's a cycle that destroys organisations from the inside out. Right now, I'm watching it happen in real time, and it's frustrating because it's preventable.

The digital transformation project is a perfect example. Aiden Cross and his team are brilliant at the technical implementation — they've delivered 70% of the planned digital services ahead of schedule and under budget. But we never built a proper change management plan for the human side. We assumed people would adapt because the technology is obviously better. That's not how change works, especially in local government where many staff joined specifically because they value face-to-face community service.

I've got outdoor crew supervisors who've been here 15 years suddenly expected to use tablets for work orders. Some are embracing it, but others feel like we're telling them their experience doesn't matter. Meanwhile, our younger staff are frustrated that basic processes still require paper forms and multiple approvals. The gap is widening instead of closing, and nobody asked me to develop a change strategy until problems started surfacing.

The housing density consultation has been equally reactive. We got the state directive and immediately went into crisis management mode, scheduling community meetings to "explain" the policy rather than genuinely engaging with resident concerns. I understand the policy is mandatory, but people can sense when consultation is performative. We're burning through community goodwill at exactly the moment we need to build trust for our digital transformation.

What really bothers me is how we're handling workforce planning. We keep hiring the same types of people for the same types of roles, then wondering why we can't attract young professionals or build digital capability. Local government has an image problem — people think it's bureaucratic, slow-moving, and career-limiting. Sometimes they're right, but we have amazing opportunities to make real community impact that private sector can't offer.

I disagree with Margaret Chen on our recruitment approach. She wants to keep advertising through traditional channels and selecting for "cultural fit," which usually means people who won't challenge existing ways of working. I think we need to actively recruit change agents and create career pathways that show progression opportunities. We're competing with organisations that offer flexible work, professional development, and meaningful projects. Our value proposition can't just be job security anymore.

## Working at Metro Council

The relationships here are complex because we're large enough to have departmental silos but small enough that everyone knows everyone's business. Helen Marchetti has been CEO for three years and genuinely tries to modernise the organisation while respecting its community service mission. She gave me the dual HR/engagement role because she recognises that internal and external relationships drive everything we do. She backs my recommendations most of the time, though she sometimes underestimates how long change management takes.

Aiden Cross and I have a productive but sometimes tense working relationship. He's incredibly competent at digital implementation and understands the technical challenges better than anyone. But he gets frustrated when staff resistance slows down project timelines. He sees it as people being stubborn or scared of change, while I see it as natural human response to poorly managed transformation. We've had several frank conversations about the difference between system adoption and user engagement.

Farah Haddad in Planning is under enormous pressure right now, and it shows in our interactions. She's dealing with the housing density controversy daily while trying to maintain planning standards and community relationships. She sometimes pushes back on consultation timelines because she needs decisions quickly, but I have to remind her that rushing engagement creates bigger problems later. We work well together when we have sufficient time to plan, but lately everything feels reactive.

Ben Abbott and I collaborate frequently on parks and sustainability projects, which tend to generate positive community engagement compared to planning issues. He understands that good consultation upfront prevents problems during implementation. The outdoor crew respects him, and he's been helpful in managing their concerns about new digital work order systems.

Margaret Chen and I clash more than I'd like, particularly around recruitment and performance management approaches. She comes from a more traditional corporate background and sometimes treats staff concerns as compliance issues rather than engagement opportunities. She's focused on risk mitigation while I'm focused on capability building. We're both right in our domains, but it creates tension in decision-making.

What I value most about working here is the genuine community mission. Despite our challenges, everyone cares about serving residents well. People get frustrated with processes and politics, but they chose local government because they want to make their community better. That shared purpose gets us through difficult periods.

## Current Challenges

Right now, I feel like I'm running crisis management on multiple fronts simultaneously. The housing density controversy has consumed enormous time and energy since the state directive came down. I'm averaging three community meetings per week, and the feedback is consistently hostile. Residents feel blindsided by the policy and suspicious of our consultation process. They're not wrong to be suspicious — we're consulting on implementation details when the fundamental decision has already been made at state level.

The community meetings have become venting sessions rather than productive engagement. I'm using every facilitation skill I have to keep them civilised, but people are genuinely angry about parking, traffic, and neighbourhood character impacts. Last week's session in Morley Heights ran 90 minutes over schedule because residents demanded to speak. I left feeling like we'd accomplished nothing except letting people express frustration.

Simultaneously, staff anxiety about digital transformation is escalating. We've got people who've worked here for decades suddenly feeling incompetent because they struggle with new systems. Others are excited about modernisation but frustrated that change is happening slowly. The age and skill gaps are creating workplace tension that didn't exist two years ago.

I'm seeing concerning patterns in our staff wellbeing metrics. Stress leave is up 23% compared to last year, with higher rates among both our oldest and youngest employees for different reasons. Exit interview feedback increasingly mentions "organisational uncertainty" and "unclear direction" alongside traditional concerns about career progression.

The workload is unsustainable for me personally. Managing HR for 320 people while running intensive community consultation requires different types of energy and attention. Some weeks I'm in back-to-back meetings from 8am to 8pm, switching between performance management conversations and heated planning discussions. I've had to delegate more training coordination and policy development than I'd like, which affects quality.

We need to hire additional community engagement capacity, but that requires budget approval and clarity about role definition. Are we investing in engagement as a strategic capability, or just addressing current crisis? Helen supports additional resources in principle, but Margaret questions whether consultation workload will remain at current levels once the density controversy settles.

I'm also worried about our recruitment pipeline for critical roles. We've been advertising for a Senior HR Officer for four months without finding suitable candidates willing to work in local government. Our digital project needs change management support that we're currently not providing. The outdoor crew needs supervisors who can bridge traditional operations with new technology requirements.

The bigger challenge is organisational identity during transformation. We're changing fundamental processes and service delivery methods while trying to maintain community connection and staff engagement. Some changes are necessary and overdue, but the pace and scope are testing our change management capacity.

Looking ahead, I see opportunities to better integrate internal and external engagement strategies, but I need organisational support and realistic timeframes. Staff who feel valued and capable deliver better community service. Residents who feel heard and respected are more likely to support necessary changes. The connections are obvious, but implementing them requires patience and resources we're currently struggling to provide.
